1:
ḥăzôn (ḥāzôn, H2377) — "vision/revelation received." Isaiah identifies his entire book as a single integrated vision, not a collection of separate oracles. This is the preferred self-description of classical Hebrew prophecy as direct visual perception rather than merely verbal reception. Compare Obadiah 1:1 and Nahum 1:1. The book of Isaiah opens with this rarer term rather than the more common dāḇār (word of the LORD), signaling that these oracles were experienced as a unified prophetic seeing.

The four-king span (Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) dates the ministry to approximately 740–700 BCE — from the height of Judean prosperity through the Assyrian crisis. This temporal anchoring is not merely biographical; it situates the vision within the arc of covenant history.
2:
šimʿû šāmayim wĕhaʾăzînî ʾereṣ — "Hear, O heavens, and give ear, O earth." Two different Hebrew verbs: šāmaʿ (hear/obey) addressed to the heavens; ʾāzan (give ear/attend closely) to the earth. The distinction is stylistic but both are legal summons vocabulary. The cosmic witnesses (heavens and earth) are invoked as parties to the Sinaitic covenant (cf. Deut 30:19, 31:28; Mic 6:1-2), establishing the rîb (lawsuit) pattern that structures this entire chapter.

pāšĕʿû (pāšaʿ, H6586) — not merely "sinned" or "erred" but deliberate, willful breach of a covenant relationship; rebellion of a vassal against a sovereign. The MKT renders "rebelled," which captures this active agency. The children (bānîm) whom YHWH reared to maturity (giḏaltî) have committed pāšaʿ — the most serious category of covenant violation.
3:
yādaʿ (H3045) — relational, experiential knowing, not merely cognitive recognition. "Israel does not know" means Israel has failed to maintain the covenant bond that constitutes relationship, not merely that Israel is ignorant of facts. An ox "knows" its owner in the sense of having an established bond of recognition and dependence.

The ox/donkey proverb is a qal wahomer (lesser-to-greater) argument: if even domesticated work animals maintain the primary bond of recognition with their owner, how much more should Israel, YHWH's covenant son, maintain theirs. ʾēbûs (H18) = "manger/feeding trough," not simply "master" — the donkey knows the source of its provision. Israel, by contrast, has stopped seeing YHWH as provider.
4:
hôy (H1945) — the exclamatory lament particle, used both as a funeral cry (alas!) and as a prophetic woe-oracle announcing judgment. Its combination with gôy ḥōṭēʾ (sinful nation) is striking: nations are never called "sinful" in Deuteronomic terms, only Israel — the one to whom covenant obligations applied. The entire verse has four accusatory phrases stacked asyndetically (without conjunctions), creating rhetorical urgency.

ʿāzĕbû = "have abandoned" (perfect, completed action); nāʾăṣû = "have spurned" (perfect). Both verbs describe completed covenant breach, not ongoing drift. nāzōrû (H2114) = "are estranged, turned away" — the third charge. The accumulation reinforces the totality of the abandonment.
5:
ʿal-māh — "Why?" / "For what?" — the rhetorical question implies that further punishment is pointless, not that YHWH is incapable; Israel has reached the point of hardened incorrigibility. The entire body-politic metaphor of vv. 5-6 presupposes a patient who has refused all treatment.

kol-rōʾš lāḥolî = "the whole head is sick/diseased (ḥolî)"; kol-lēḇāḇ dawwāy = "the whole heart is faint/failing (dāwway)." The pairing of head (leadership/mind) and heart (will/center) as both diseased signals comprehensive moral and social corruption from top to bottom.
6:
The body metaphor from v. 5 is extended with clinical specificity: pĕṣaʿ = wound/laceration; ḥabbûrâ = bruise/welt; makkâ ṭĕriyyâ = open/fresh sore. All three denote different stages or types of injury, suggesting the comprehensive and untreated nature of the wound. Not merely injured but unattended.

lōʾ-zōrû wĕlōʾ ḥubbāšû = "not pressed out, and not bound up" — wound treatment in ancient medicine involved squeezing out infected material, then bandaging. wĕlōʾ rukkeḵāh baššāmen = "and not softened with oil." All three remedial actions absent. The metaphor describes not the absence of suffering but the absence of any attempted cure.
7:
šĕmāmâ (H8077) — desolation, the word that names what is left after total devastation. Structurally related to the threat-language of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 (covenant curses for covenant breach). Isaiah is describing the covenant curses coming to pass, not merely a political situation.

kĕmaḥpēkat zārîm = "like the overthrow of strangers" — a disputed phrase; the MKT "a desolation like the ruin left by invaders" captures the comparison to devastation wrought by foreign armies. The land belongs to Israel but looks like it has been thoroughly colonized by outsiders.
8:
bat-ṣiyyôn — "daughter of Zion" — the city of Jerusalem personified as a daughter/young woman. This is a standard Hebrew personification idiom (cf. Lamentations 1-2). The emotional weight is grief for a beloved person, not just a place.

sûkkâ = "temporary shelter/booth" — the agricultural harvest booth (related to the Feast of Booths, sukkot), flimsy and impermanent; mĕlûnâ = watchman's hut in a cucumber field — equally temporary. nĕṣûrâ = "besieged city." The three images move from vineyard to field to military siege — Jerusalem is isolated, exposed, temporary, as if already under attack.
9:
śārîd (H8300) = "survivor, remnant" — a crucial theological term in Isaiah (occurs repeatedly in chs. 1, 4, 6, 10, 11, 28, 37). The "tiny surviving remnant" (kiṁʿāṭ = "almost," with śārîd) is the sole basis for Israel's continued existence. Theologically: remnant is not a consolation prize but the seed of continuity.

The Sodom-Gomorrah comparison is jarring: those cities were the canonical example of total divine destruction (Gen 18-19). That Israel "would have been like Sodom" but for the remnant — not that Israel was like Sodom in wickedness, but in deserved fate — is a statement of sheer grace. The condition of survival is not merit but mercy.
10: The address to Jerusalem's leaders as "rulers of Sodom" (qĕṣînê Sĕdôm) and the people as "people of Gomorrah" is a rhetorical shock tactic — v. 9 had just said Israel was almost like Sodom in its destruction; now Isaiah says the leaders ARE Sodom-like in their character. The rîb lawsuit continues: šimʿû (hear, imperative) and haʾăzînû (give ear, imperative) repeat v. 2's legal summons, now directed at Judah's leadership specifically.
11:
māh-lî rōb-ziḇḥêkem — "What to me is the multitude of your sacrifices?" — rhetorical question dismissing the cult. Māh-lî = "what is it to me?" — a formula of total disinterest (cf. Matt 8:29). The verse does not condemn sacrifice per se but sacrifice divorced from obedience (cf. 1 Sam 15:22; Hos 6:6; Amos 5:21-24). This is the prophetic critique: the sacrificial system was never intended to substitute for covenant loyalty.

śābaʿtî = "I am surfeited, I have had more than enough" (perfect of śāḇaʿ); lōʾ ḥāpaṣtî = "I do not desire/take pleasure in" (ḥāpēṣ = to delight in, take pleasure). YHWH's response to the temple cult is not theological interest but visceral revulsion — the strongest language of divine rejection.
12:
mî-biqēš zōʾt miyyĕdekem — "Who required this from your hands?" — bāqaš = to seek, require, demand. The question is devastating in its simplicity: no one asked for empty ritual. The rhetorical form implies the answer: YHWH himself did not require this; what he required was justice and righteousness (v. 17). The ritual came to substitute for what was actually demanded.

rĕmōs ḥăṣērāy = "to trample through my courts" — the temple precincts (ḥăṣēr) are being polluted rather than honored by feet that bring no matching moral state. The image of trampling (used for enemy armies destroying cities in other contexts) applied to worshipers is deliberately provocative.
13:
lōʾ tôsîpû hāḇîʾ minḥat-šāwĕʾ — "Stop bringing worthless offerings." Minḥat-šāwĕʾ = "offering of emptiness/vanity" — šāwĕʾ (H7723) means nothingness, emptiness, falsehood (the same root as the third commandment: "take the name of the LORD in vain"). The offerings are themselves defined as šāwĕʾ because they are offered by those whose lives contradict them.

qĕṭōret tôʿēḇâ = "incense is an abomination (tôʿēḇâ)" — tôʿēḇâ is the strongest ritual-disgust word in Hebrew, used for idolatry (Deut 7:25), sexual immorality (Lev 18:22), and unjust weights (Prov 11:1). Applying it to incense — prescribed in the law itself — is Isaiah's most radical statement: a commanded ritual can become an abomination when separated from covenant faithfulness.
14:
śānēʾtî (perfect of śānēʾ, to hate) — "I utterly hate." The perfective aspect emphasizes this as a settled state of divine disposition, not a momentary reaction. YHWH hates the festivals, new moons (ḥodšêkem), and appointments (môʿădêkem) — the full calendar of Levitically mandated gatherings. The word môʿēd (appointed time/festival) is the same word used in Leviticus 23 for the sacred calendar. The institutions are not condemned; their divorce from covenant life is.

hāyû ʿālay lĕṭōraḥ = "they have become a burden to me" (ṭōraḥ = burden, travail). The image is ironic: Israel was supposed to be YHWH's treasured possession (sĕgullâ); instead the relationship has become a burden. nillêtî nĕśōʾ = "I am weary of carrying them" — the festivals have become a burden YHWH must sustain rather than an offering of worship.
15:
bĕpārĕśkem kappêkem — "when you spread out your palms/hands" — the gesture of supplication, hands extended outward and upward. Kappayim = palms (the hollow of the hand). The image is of the universal gesture of prayer — yet YHWH responds by hiding his eyes (maʿlîm ʿênay = "I will hide my eyes, avert my gaze").

yĕdêkem dāmîm māĕʾû — "your hands are full of blood (dāmîm)." The plural dāmîm = bloodshed/bloodguilt (distinguished from dām = blood in general). The hands raised in prayer are the same hands covered in the blood of the innocent (social injustice, exploitation). This is the reason the prayers go unanswered: the posture of prayer and the posture of oppression cannot coexist.
16:
rāḥăṣû hizzakkû — "wash (rāḥaṣ) and make yourselves clean (zāḵāh)" — two imperatives, moving from external to internal: ritual washing and moral cleansing. Rāḥaṣ is the word for priestly ablutions; zāḵāh has broader moral-purity connotations. The sequence suggests that external ritual without internal transformation is the fundamental problem of vv. 11-15.

sûrû rōaʿ maʿalĕlêkem — "remove (sûr) your evil deeds from before my eyes" — sûr = to turn aside, remove; the deeds must be actively removed, not merely regretted. The phrase miggneged ʿênāy = "from before my eyes" reverses v. 15 where YHWH hid his eyes from their prayers; now removal of evil results in restored divine attention.
17:
lĭmdû hêṭêb = "learn to do good" — the positive form of v. 16's negative commands. Lāmad (to learn) implies that doing good requires instruction and practice, not mere intention. dĭršû mišpāṭ = "seek/pursue justice" (dāraš = to seek with urgency, to investigate thoroughly). ʾaššĕrû ḥāmôṣ = "correct the oppressor" — a difficult phrase; ʾāšar can mean "to make straight/correct" and ḥāmôṣ = "oppressor/extortioner."

šipṭû yātôm rîbû ʾalmānâ = "defend/judge the orphan, take up the widow's cause (rîb)." The yātôm (orphan) and ʾalmānâ (widow) are the canonical vulnerable pair in OT social law (Exod 22:22; Deut 10:18; 14:29; 24:17-21; Ps 68:5; 146:9). Their protection is the concrete test of covenant justice, not temple attendance.
18:
lĕkû-nāʾ wĕniwwākĕḥâ = "Come now and let us reason together/argue the case" — yākaḥ (H3198) is a legal-forensic term: to dispute a case, to bring a charge, to adjudicate. The divine invitation to "reason together" is a courtroom summons, not merely a friendly conversation. YHWH invites Israel to state their defense.

yihyû kaššāleg yalbînû = "they shall become white as snow (šeleg)." Šānî and tôlaʿat (scarlet/crimson) = the red dye from the scale insect (Kermes biblicus) — a costly, deep, essentially permanent colorfast dye. Against this: šeleg (snow) and ṣemer (wool) = the paradigm of whiteness. The contrast of maximum color saturation against maximum whiteness makes this the most vivid forgiveness image in the OT.
19:
ʾim-tōʾbû wĕšĕmaʿtem = "if you are willing (tōʾbû, from ʾābâ, to be willing/agree) and obey (šāmaʿ, to hear/obey)." Both ʾābâ and šāmaʿ are volitional: the first is the inner disposition, the second the outward compliance. The condition is both attitude and action.

ṭûb hāʾāreṣ tōʾkēlû = "the best/goodness of the land you shall eat." The blessing formula echoes Deuteronomy's prosperity language — the land as gift conditional on obedience (Deut 28:1-14). The obedience-blessing framework is operative throughout this pericope.
20:
ʾim-tĕmaʾănû wûmĕrîtem = "if you refuse (māʾēn) and resist (mārar)" — the two negatives matching the two positives of v. 19. Māʾēn = to refuse, decline (volitional); mārar = to be rebellious/bitter — the root of Miriam and Meribah.

ḥereḇ tĕʾukkĕlû = "by the sword you shall be eaten/devoured" — the same verb ʾākal (eat) used in both the blessing (v. 19: you shall eat) and the curse (v. 20: you shall be eaten). The covenant structure of blessing and curse is embedded in the very grammar. Pî YHWH dibbēr = "for the mouth of the LORD has spoken" — the oracular confirmation formula that seals the pronouncement.
21:
ʾêkâh hāyĕtâ lĕzônâ = "How has she become a harlot (zônâ)!" — the lament particle ʾêkâh (alas/how) opens the dirge form (cf. Lamentations 1:1, 2:1, 4:1 — same word). The prophetic move from lawsuit to lament is abrupt and emotionally powerful. The city that was "faithful" (neʾĕmānâ) has become a zônâ — spiritual prostitution, covenant infidelity in its most degraded form.

mišpāṭ/ṣedeq — the twin pillars of the ideal city: justice (right social order) and righteousness (conformity to divine standard). Their replacement by murderers (mĕrṣāḥîm) states the case with maximum compression: the city that was characterized by mišpāṭ and ṣedeq now has neither.
22:
kaspĕkā hāyâ lĕsîgîm = "your silver has become dross (sîgîm)." Sîgîm = slag, the waste product left after smelting — what is removed when refining silver, the opposite of the pure metal. The metaphor of metallurgical debasement (pure silver → dross; fine wine → diluted water) describes moral adulteration with precision: not ruined from outside but corrupted from within.

sōbĕʾăkā māhûl bammāyim = "your finest wine (sōbēʾ) is diluted (māhûl) with water." Māhûl from the root māhal = to dilute, to adulterate. The pairing of adulterated silver and adulterated wine introduces the theme of the next verses: corrupt leaders who have debased justice as thoroughly as a merchant adulterates a product.
23:
šōrĕrîm = "rebels/revolters" — from šārar (to be hostile, to be an adversary); ḥaḇrê gaḇḇāʾîm = "companions of thieves (gaḇ)." The rulers are characterized not merely as corrupt but as partners in crime — their position as judges makes their complicity in theft the more damning.

kullô ʾōhēḇ šōḥad = "every one loves a bribe (šōḥad)" — šōḥad is specifically the bribe that perverts legal judgment (cf. Deut 10:17; 16:19; Exod 23:8). wĕrōdēp šalmōnîm = "chasing after gifts/payoffs" (šalmōnîm = rewards/remunerations). The orphan (yātôm) and widow (ʾalmānâ) of v. 17 resurface: the same vulnerable groups YHWH commanded to protect are those the rulers actively fail.
24:
hāʾādôn YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾăḇîr yiśrāʾēl — three divine titles stacked: hāʾādôn (the Lord, sovereign), YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt (LORD of armies/hosts), ʾăḇîr yiśrāʾēl (Mighty One/Champion of Israel — ʾaḇîr = bull/warrior). The threefold title makes this the most solemn divine pronouncement in the chapter; this is YHWH in his full covenant authority.

ʾinnāḥēm (hithpael of nāḥam) = "I will relieve/console myself" — the reflexive form: YHWH relieves himself of the burden of his enemies (cf. v. 14: the festivals have been a burden). The word can also mean "comfort" (YHWH takes comfort in acting against evil) — the irony: the consolation that comes from punishing oppressors mirrors Israel's need for consolation.
25:
bōr (H1253) = lye, potash, a strong alkali used in ancient smelting to separate dross from metal. The refining metaphor returns (cf. v. 22: silver → dross): YHWH will apply bōr to Israel as a metallurgist refines silver, removing impurities (sîgîm = dross). The judgment is purposeful, not merely punitive — the goal is purification.

wĕʾāsîrâ kol-ʾabddîlêk = "and I will remove all your alloy/impurities" — bĕdîl (H913) is specifically the tin or lead mixed into silver to create alloy; smelting removes it. The metaphor of smelting-as-judgment is pervasive in Hebrew prophecy (Jer 6:29; Ezek 22:18-22; Mal 3:2-3) and always implies a purified remnant on the other side of the furnace.
26:
wĕʾāšîḇâ šōpĕṭayik kĕḇārīʾšōnâ = "I will restore your judges as at the first" — the kĕ- (as/like) comparison points to the ideal period of Mosaic administration, not merely political stability. Šāpaṭ here points to the combined function of judge-deliverer (as in the book of Judges), not merely a juridical officer.

qirěyat haṣṣedeq hāʾîr hanneʾĕmānâ — "the City of Righteousness (ṣedeq), the Faithful City (neʾĕmānâ)" — the terms of the ideal city in v. 21 (where she was called faithful) are fully restored. The judgment of vv. 24-25 is not termination but restoration: refining achieves what punishment could not — the city's original character.
27: ṣiyyôn bĕmišpāṭ tiggāʾēl = "Zion will be redeemed through/by justice (bĕmišpāṭ)." The preposition bĕ here is instrumental: justice is the means of redemption, not merely its product. Gāʾal (H1350) = kinsman-redeemer action — the technical term for the near-kinsman who rescues family members from slavery, debt, or death (cf. Ruth 3-4; Lev 25). YHWH acting as kinsman-redeemer for Zion is one of Isaiah's major theological claims (cf. Isa 41:14; 43:14; 44:6; 47:4; 54:5).
28: wĕšeber pōšĕʿîm wĕḥaṭṭāʾîm yaḥdāw = "and the crushing/breaking (šeḇer) of rebels and sinners together." Pōšĕʿîm (rebels, those who commit pāšaʿ — deliberate covenant breach, v. 2) and ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners, those who miss the mark). Both categories are destroyed together. wĕʿōzĕḇê YHWH yiklû = "and those who forsake the LORD will be consumed (kālâ)." The judgment is total for those in the second category; the remnant (v. 9) is for those who respond to the invitation of vv. 16-19.
29:
kî yēḇōšû mēʾêlîm ʾăšer ḥămaḏtem = "for you will be put to shame (bôš) because of the sacred oaks (ʾêlîm) you desired (ḥāmad)." ʾÊlîm (H352 + H356) are sacred oak trees used in Canaanite fertility cult worship (cf. Hos 4:13; Ezek 6:13). ḥāmad = to covet, desire intensely — the same root as the tenth commandment (do not covet). The desire for sacred trees is covenant-violation in the mode of coveting.

gannôt = "ritual gardens" — gardens associated with fertility rites (cf. Isa 65:3; 66:17). The shame (bôš) will be commensurate with the desire: the things Israel pursued will become the occasion of their disgrace.
30:
kĕʾallâ nōḇelet ʿălêhā = "like an oak tree with falling/withering leaves (nōḇelet)" — nāḇal = to wilt, fade, fall (related to the word for corpse, the foolish person, and the wineskin that deflates). The sacred oak (ʾêlâ = terebinth/oak, v. 29) becomes the image of Israel's own fate — the tree they worshipped becomes the metaphor for their withering.

wĕkĕgannâ ʾăšer mayim ʾên lāh = "like a garden without water" — the ritual gardens of v. 29 now lack the water that gives life. The irony: Israel pursued fertility cult practices at watered sacred groves; their judgment is to become a waterless garden. The judgment mirrors the sin precisely.
31:
wĕhāyâ heḥāsôn lĕniṣôreṯ = "and the powerful one (ḥāsôn) will become tinder (niṣôreṯ)" — ḥāsôn (H2626) = strong, powerful; niṣôreṯ (H5369 or H5534) = tinder, something that easily catches fire. The most powerful person becomes the most combustible — power without covenant alignment becomes fuel for judgment.

û-pōʿălô lĕniṣôṣ = "and his work (pōʿal) a spark (niṣôṣ)." The person and their deeds burn together simultaneously. wĕšārĕpû šĕnêhem yaḥdāw wĕʾên mĕkaббeh = "and both will burn together with no one to put them out" — the self-consuming judgment: the oppressor and his works destroy each other.
